text: The surgeon visually identifies the pancreas and proceeds to grasp the perigastric tissue using surgical instruments.
The surgeon removes the peripancreatic tissue, carefully dissecting it away from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs an extended dissection around the portal hepatis, focusing on the area due to the presence of early-stage esophageal cancer.
The surgeon performs dissection around the common hepatic artery, carefully separating surrounding tissue.
The surgeon performs a moderate dissection around the common hepatic artery, carefully navigating the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon exposes lymph nodes near the common hepatic artery, which may also be labeled as celiac lymph nodes.
The surgeon points out lymph nodes located around the hepatic artery, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the left gastric artery, visible in the surgical field, as part of the ongoing dissection.
The surgeon creates a space and performs dissection along the long axis, focusing on the area around the common hepatic artery.
The surgeon encircles the artery using webbing, demonstrating the technique with visible precision.
The surgeon completes the dissection along the celiac axis, revealing the cleared tissue around the common hepatic artery, splenic artery, and celiac axis.
The surgeon prepares to use a green stapler for the procedure, indicating a preference over the blue stapler for this step.
The surgeon positions a SureForm stapler to create a long graft, ensuring a large circumference is achieved before firing the stapler for the first time.
The surgeon holds up the omentum and pulls the lymphatic tissue upwards, positioning the stapler underneath the omental tissue to remove the lymph nodes surrounding the stomach.
The surgeon pulls the stomach to extend its length, preparing to create a long graft for the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs dissection of the right recurrent laryngeal nerve lobe, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues in the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon highlights the vagus nerve, visible in the surgical field, as they begin the initial steps of the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon exposes the vagus nerve and opens the mediastinal pleura along the subclavian artery, revealing the underlying artery and nerve.
The surgeon uses the third arm of the robotic system to hold the mediastinal pleura and pull it towards the anterior side, effectively widening the surgical field in the mediastinum.

The surgeon performs gentle dissection along the vagus nerve, carefully navigating around branches of nerves and vessels. The recurrent laryngeal nerve is visible, requiring meticulous dissection to avoid damage.

The surgeon identifies the area between the recurrent phalangeal lobe and the esophagus, which is a common site for cancer metastasis. The goal is to remove the entire tissue between the nerve and the esophagus.
The surgeon identifies the recurrent rhizome to prevent nerve injury, then proceeds to remove surrounding tissues carefully to avoid damage to the nerve.
The surgeon exposes lymph nodes around the nerve, identifying the top pet lymph nodes in the right upper thoracic area.
The surgeon follows the recurrent laryngeal nerve, identifying and dividing vascular branches while exposing the nerve and adjacent lymph nodes.

In the neck area, the surgeon identifies arteries crossing the surgical field, likely including the inferior thyroid artery. The thyroid gland is visible as the surgeon dissects deeper into the tissue.

The surgeon focuses on removing tissue between the esophagus and recurrent laryngeal nerve, extending from the chest to the neck. The lymph nodes in this area are identified as cervical paraesophageal lymph nodes, distinct from thoracic lymph nodes.
The surgeon identifies a lymph node in the cervical region, which is classified as one of the six arms in the Japanese lymph node classification system.
The final view shows the esophagus fully separated, with all lymph nodes removed along the left and right recurrent laryngeal nerves. The trachea and major airways remain undamaged.

The surgeon explores the left hilar region, confirming that the left pleura remains intact. The diaphragmatic lymph nodes have been completely removed, and the thoracic duct is undamaged. The stomach is then pulled up into view.

The surgeon visually assesses the suture site between the resected esophagus and the stomach graft conduit, adjusting the position of the graft to ensure proper tension and alignment without resistance.
